title: Sharepoint Sync Trick: Give Claude a Map of Your Files
tagline: Claude can't read your Sharepoint files — but it can work with a directory tree. One terminal command solves the context gap.
content:
Claude has no native access to your Sharepoint or local drives. But there's a lightweight workaround that takes 30 seconds and dramatically improves the relevance of Claude's outputs when you're working with internal documents.

Open Windows Terminal and run this command:

tree "C:\Users\YourUsername\Legends\Legends Global Insights - Documents\Insights Team" /F > sharepoint_tree.txt

This outputs a full directory map — every folder and filename — into a plain text file at C:\Users\username\sharepoint_tree.txt. Feed that file into Claude at the start of your session.

**What this gives you:** Claude won't know what's inside those files, but it will know what exists. It can reference filenames, suggest which documents are likely relevant to your task, and avoid asking you for files that don't exist. It's a pseudo-integration until full file connectivity is built out.

**Practical limits to keep in mind:** This works best for teams with clean, descriptive file naming conventions. If your Sharepoint looks like "Final_v3_REVISED_USE THIS ONE.xlsx", the tree won't help much. It's also a snapshot — you'll want to regenerate it when the directory changes significantly.

The same `tree` command works for any local directory. Run it on your project folders, your data exports, your analysis outputs — anywhere you want Claude to have structural awareness without full file access.

title: How to Turn Claude into a Senior Data Analyst (With a Single Prompt)
tagline: A structured analytical framework prompt that transforms raw data uploads into executive-ready insights — with statistical rigour built in.
content:
Most people upload a dataset and ask Claude "what do you see?" The output is generic. Here's a prompt framework that forces a structured, business-grade analysis every time.

**The framework covers four mandatory layers:**

**1. Data Overview** — Before any analysis, Claude audits the dataset: dimensions, data types, time periods covered, missing values, outliers, sampling bias, and an overall data completeness score (% of usable records). This step catches quality issues before they corrupt conclusions.

**2. Key Statistics** — Full descriptive statistics (mean, median, mode, standard deviation, percentiles), correlation patterns, distribution analysis, and normality tests. Every anomaly gets flagged with a confidence interval — not just noted.

**3. Insights & Findings** — 3 to 5 business insights ranked by impact potential, each backed by statistical evidence and effect sizes. Trends, patterns, and unexpected findings. Uncertainty levels stated explicitly.

**4. Recommendations** — Data-driven next steps with expected ROI, implementation timeline, and gaps in the current dataset that would strengthen the analysis.

**How to deploy this:** Paste the framework as a system prompt before uploading your data file. Then simply write: "Analyse the data I provided using this framework."

**Why it works:** The structure prevents Claude from jumping to conclusions. The explicit instruction to flag anomalies before finalising output is particularly valuable — it surfaces data quality issues that would otherwise only appear after you've already built a report on bad foundations.

**Pro tip:** Add a line at the end specifying your audience — "All outputs are for presentation to the Head of Finance" — and Claude will calibrate its language accordingly without you needing to edit the output.

title: Prompt Shortcuts: The Command Library That Saves 20 Minutes Per Session
tagline: A curated set of slash-command shortcuts that anchor Claude's behaviour — from output format to reasoning depth to tone. Copy, adapt, use.
content:
Claude responds to structural cues placed at the beginning of a prompt. These shortcuts exploit that behaviour to get consistently better outputs without rewriting your prompt from scratch each time.

**The shortcuts are organised into five categories:**

**Simplification** — /ELI5 (explain like I'm five), /TLDR (concise summary), /BRIEFLY (short direct answer), /EXEC SUMMARY (executive-style key points).

**Structure** — /STEP-BY-STEP (sequential reasoning), /CHECKLIST (actionable list), /FORMAT AS (table, JSON, markdown), /SCHEMA (structured data model), /ROLE: TASK: FORMAT: (the most powerful trio — define persona, objective, and output format in one instruction).

**Persona** — /ACT AS (assign a role), /JARGON (domain-specific vocabulary), /AUDIENCE (adapt for a specific reader), /TONE (formal, urgent, analytical), /DEV MODE, /PM MODE.

**Analysis** — /COMPARE (side-by-side), /SWOT, /MULTI-PERSPECTIVE, /FIRST PRINCIPLES, /PITFALLS (surface edge cases proactively), /METRICS MODE (quantify everything).

**Quality control** — /CHAIN OF THOUGHT (show reasoning steps), /NO AUTOPILOT (ban generic outputs), /EVAL-SELF (self-critique), /GUARDRAIL (set hard limits).

**Three rules for using these effectively:**

First, position matters — put shortcuts at the very start of your prompt. Claude processes sequentially; the opening frames everything that follows.

Second, stack sparingly — two or three compatible commands work well. Five or six creates contradictions. /ELI5 and /JARGON in the same prompt will produce confused output.

Third, shortcuts amplify context — they don't replace it. /EXEC SUMMARY on a vague prompt produces a vague executive summary. The quality of your context determines the quality of the output; the shortcut determines its shape.

title: The Sketchnote Prompt: Turn Any Dense Topic into a Visual Summary
tagline: A templated prompt that converts complex subjects into professional graphic-recording-style visuals. Four variables, zero manual editing.
content:
Dense topics — model parameters, compliance frameworks, pipeline architecture — resist traditional documentation. This prompt converts them into hand-drawn sketchnote visuals that work for presentations, social content, and onboarding materials.

**The prompt template:**

"First, use well-constructed analogies to clarify [TOPIC]. Relate its principles to everyday experiences. Based on that explanation, create a hand-drawn sketchnote visual summary using: black ink fine-liners for outlines, [ACCENT COLOR] for shading, [TEXT STYLE] text, radially distributed doodles and business icons around a 3D-style central title box, arrows connecting ideas, aspect ratio [RATIO]."

**The four variables you control:**

**Topic** — free text. Be specific: "GenAI parameters vs. content size" performs better than "AI concepts".

**Text style** — Telegraphic (nouns and verbs only, extreme brevity), Punchy (short high-impact sentences), Scannable (headers and bolded keywords), Nugget-sized (standalone bite-sized facts), Expansive (full explanations).

**Accent color** — Teal, Orange, Muted Red, Emerald, Navy Blue, Mustard, Charcoal, Royal Purple. These eight are pre-tested against black ink line art.

**Aspect ratio** — 16:9 for presentations, 9:16 for Stories and Shorts, 1:1 for social posts, 3:4 for portrait documents.

**Why the radial composition instruction matters:** It prevents the output from defaulting to a linear list with icons. "Radially distributed" around a central title produces the whiteboard brainstorm feel — ideas branching outward, connected by arrows, with visual hierarchy that reads at a glance.

**Practical use cases:** Onboarding explainers ("How our data pipeline works" + Scannable + Navy + 16:9), conference slides ("Zero Trust Architecture" + Telegraphic + Charcoal + 16:9), social content ("Why sleep matters" + Punchy + Orange + 1:1).
